context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with my philosophy assignment about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I’m pretty confused about how act consequentialism handles the trolley-style cases, especially stuff like Fuel and Blood.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the core rule. For an act consequentialist, what makes an act right or wrong in a particular case: the kind of act it is, or the overall consequences it produces?

If you can state that in one sentence, then apply it to one case first—say a basic trolley case. What would that view say, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay focused on the assigned philosophy exercise and use a Socratic, guided-discovery approach. Since the student is confused about act consequentialism and specific cases, I should not explain everything outright or give a ready-made solution. Instead, I will narrow the task, ask a question that moves directly toward the core principle of act consequentialism, and then connect that principle to the cases the student named. I will keep the response brief and avoid writing any part of the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I think for an act consequentialist, an act is right if it produces the best overall consequences in that specific situation, not because of the type of act it is. So in a basic trolley case, I think they’d say it’s okay to divert the trolley if that saves five and only one dies, because 5 saved is a better outcome than 1 saved.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now test your principle for consistency.

If act consequentialism only asks which available act has the best outcome, what should it say about Fuel and Blood? Should those come out the same as the basic trolley case, or differently?

Pick one of those cases and tell me which outcome the view would favor.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core act consequentialist principle and applied it correctly to a basic trolley case. To advance their understanding without giving the answer, I should now push them to test whether that same reasoning applies consistently across the lecture cases, especially Fuel and Blood. This keeps the discussion moving toward the assignment’s comparative and evaluative parts while remaining bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think it should probably treat them the same if the numbers and outcomes are the same. So for Blood, I guess an act consequentialist would still say killing one to save five is the right act, if that really does produce the best overall result.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key pressure point. If the view treats Blood like trolley diversion, do you think that matches your own moral judgment?

Which case feels harder to permit, and what difference between the cases seems morally important to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by applying act consequentialism uniformly. To deepen understanding and move toward the assignment’s evaluative component, I should now get them to articulate the tension: many people judge some cases differently despite identical numbers. I will ask a targeted question that pushes them to compare cases and identify whether act consequentialism seems too permissive, without supplying the conclusion. This remains Socratic, concise, and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: No, not really — Blood feels way harder for me to permit than just diverting a trolley. I think the difference is that in Blood, the one person seems more directly used as a means to save the five, and that feels morally worse than just redirecting a threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re moving beyond act consequentialism.

Try to turn that intuition into a rule: when, if ever, is killing one permissible if the person is not being used as a tool, versus when the person is being used as a means?

Can you state a rough principle in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a morally relevant distinction—using a person as a means versus redirecting a threat. I should reinforce that insight and push them to formulate a general principle from it, since the assignment asks for a general account. I must avoid naming or stating a finished theory for them, and instead ask a focused question that helps them turn the intuition into a rule they can defend.

exchanges.student: Maybe the rough rule is that killing one person can be morally permissible to save five when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect, but not when I’m using that person as the means for saving the others. I’m not totally sure if that’s too simplistic, but it seems closer to my intuition about why trolley diversion feels different from Blood.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong draft principle. Don’t polish it yet—test it.

Take two cases: one where your rule says ‘permissible’ and one where it says ‘not permissible.’ For each, ask: is the one person’s death a side effect, or part of the plan?

Which two cases from Lecture 13 would be best for that test?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated a promising principle. To help them evaluate and refine it, I should have them test it against the assignment's cases rather than improve the wording for them. This supports guided discovery, keeps the answer brief, and moves directly toward the paper’s need to assess whether the proposed general account handles Fuel, Blood, and trolley variants better than act consequentialism.

exchanges.student: I think Basic Trolley would be the “permissible” one, because the one person’s death is more like a side effect of redirecting the trolley. And Blood seems like the “not permissible” one, because the person’s death is part of the plan to save the five. I’m a little unsure about Fuel though, since I don’t fully remember whether that one is supposed to count as using the person as a means.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—use Fuel to stress-test your rule.

Ask yourself: in Fuel, does the one person’s death itself help bring about the saving of the five, or would the five be saved even if that person somehow survived?

Which way you answer that tells you whether Fuel looks like Blood or Basic Trolley. What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a workable principle and has applied it to Basic Trolley and Blood. Their uncertainty about Fuel is productive, so I should not resolve the case for them. Instead, I should direct them to reconstruct Fuel by asking the exact diagnostic question their principle requires: does the person's death do causal work in saving the five, or is it a byproduct? This keeps the discussion on the assignment, uses Socratic questioning, and avoids giving a solution.

exchanges.student: I think the key question is whether the person’s death is doing the work. If the five would be saved even if that person somehow survived, then Fuel should be more like Basic Trolley; but if the death itself is what makes the rescue possible, then it seems more like Blood. I’m leaning toward Fuel being closer to Basic Trolley, but I’m not fully confident.
exchanges.tutor:
That uncertainty is fine if you can defend your reading.

Now step back: what is the main disagreement between act consequentialism and your emerging view? In one case, where would they give different verdicts, and why?

State that contrast as clearly as you can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is using the right diagnostic but remains uncertain. I should avoid settling the interpretation for them and instead move them toward the assignment's bigger picture: compare act consequentialism with their emerging alternative and evaluate which handles the cases better. A concise question prompting them to state the contrast will help them organize their argument without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I think the main disagreement is that act consequentialism only looks at which action has the best total outcome, while my view also cares about how the outcome is produced. So in a case like Blood, act consequentialism would probably say killing one to save five is right, but my view says it’s not okay if the one person is being used as a means rather than just harmed as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. That’s the core contrast.

Next question: why is your view better than act consequentialism, not just different? What does it explain about our judgments in Blood, and does it still preserve the permissibility of Basic Trolley?

Give me your best two-part defense.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the central contrast needed for the assignment. To keep guiding without giving a solution, I should now push them toward evaluation: does their view handle the cases better, and what objection might remain? This advances their argument structure while staying concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think it’s better because it explains why Blood feels morally wrong even if the numbers come out better — it captures the idea that there’s something especially objectionable about intentionally using a person as a tool. And it still preserves the permissibility of Basic Trolley, because there the one person’s death isn’t the means of saving the five, just a foreseeable side effect of stopping a bigger harm.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You now have an argument.

Final step: organize it into 3 parts—(1) what act consequentialism says, (2) why that seems wrong in Blood, (3) your better general rule.

Before you write, what’s one possible objection to your rule? For example, is “side effect vs means” always clear?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the main pieces of a defensible position: the act consequentialist principle, its verdicts on cases, and a rival principle distinguishing means from side effects. I should not draft the essay or provide a finished answer. The best next step is to have the student assemble these pieces into a simple structure and anticipate one objection, which moves directly toward completing the assignment while keeping the interaction bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that seems like a real objection — the line between a side effect and a means might be blurry in some cases, especially something like Fuel. I’m worried my rule sounds intuitive, but maybe it gets messy when I try to apply it consistently.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a serious objection, and it can improve your paper.

Ask yourself: does the existence of hard borderline cases show your rule is useless, or just that moral judgment can be indeterminate in some cases?

Which of those two claims do you want to defend?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a productive stage: they have a thesis, supporting contrast, and now a genuine objection. I should help them strengthen the argument by having them respond to that objection themselves, not by supplying a ready-made fix. A concise Socratic prompt that asks whether some blurriness undermines the rule entirely or merely limits confidence in hard cases will move them toward a more nuanced conclusion without writing for them.
